articles.title: AIEWF Daily Dispatch: Loops, Software Factories & Forward Deployed Engineers
articles.summary: On Tuesday at the AI Engineer World's Fair, there was a lot of talk about loops, agent engineering, and the emergence of software factories. Also a hot topic: open models.

articles.title: Ahmad Osman on why local AI is catching up
articles.summary: After two packed AIEWF workshops, Ahmad Osman makes the case that local AI is catching up fast — from laptops and phones to enterprise-grade infrastructure.

articles.title: The Real Question to Ask About AI Governance

articles.title: [AINews] not much happened today
articles.summary: a quiet day before the storm.

articles.title: Colocation data centers: The infrastructure race behind AI
articles.summary: Data center capital expenditures are surging as AI workloads grow, yet competitiveness increasingly depends on securing advanced accelerators, reliable electricity, and fast project delivery.

articles.title: Talking with Daniel Marovitz, SVP of Fintech at Booking.com
articles.summary: Booking.com’s senior vice president of fintech describes how the travel-booking company has become the global platform of choice by managing complexity and easing customers’ pain points.

articles.title: Solar power: From intermittent sunshine to reliable power
articles.summary: Solar photovoltaic projects with battery energy storage systems are moving beyond low-cost generation by transforming intermittent output into more reliable, higher-value electricity.

articles.title: EAF steel: Beyond the blast furnace
articles.summary: Global steel investment is flat, but DRI-EAF production is gaining share in advanced economies.

articles.title: Author Talks: Why we need a ‘ritual renaissance’
articles.summary: What if the antidote to loneliness isn’t technology but ritual? Traveling across 16 countries, author Bruce Feiler uncovered a quiet global movement helping people reconnect in an age of digital overload.

articles.title: Nuclear power: A renaissance in the making
articles.summary: The explosion of AI data centers and the continued electrification of automobiles and other products have greatly increased demand for electricity, sparking renewed interest in producing nuclear power.
